text.title: python-web-frameworks Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable selection rules for picking Django vs FastAPI vs Flask and freezing the decision in constitution.md.

rule.statement: Choose Django when the project needs a built-in admin/CMS, has heavy ORM coverage of a relational schema, requires Django's mature auth and middleware ecosystem, OR ships templated HTML pages alongside APIs.
rule.rationale: Django's `admin`, `auth`, `migrations`, `forms`, and CSRF/XSS protection are batteries-included. Reproducing them on FastAPI or Flask is typically weeks of work — and the substitutes (SQLAdmin, FastAPI-Users, custom CSRF) cover only fragments of the surface.

rule.statement: Choose FastAPI when the workload is high-concurrency I/O-bound async (ML model serving, WebSocket fanout, microservices fronting external APIs), OR auto-generated OpenAPI is a hard requirement, OR Pydantic validation is the validation strategy of record.
rule.rationale: FastAPI is async-native on Starlette and pairs with SQLAlchemy 2 async drivers. Pydantic v2 validation pipeline produces validated request bodies and OpenAPI schemas from the same source of truth.

rule.statement: Choose Flask only when the deliverable is a small internal tool, a throwaway prototype, OR a custom architecture where every Django/FastAPI convention would be discarded anyway. If you find yourself adding Flask-RESTX + Flask-Migrate + Flask-Login + Flask-WTF, stop and switch to Django.
rule.rationale: Flask is a WSGI micro-framework. Its strength is minimal core surface. The moment the extension list mirrors Django's batteries, Flask becomes pure friction.

rule.statement: In Django, async views must call ORM exclusively through `async`/`a`-prefixed methods (`User.objects.aall()`, `aget()`, `afirst()`) or wrap sync calls in `sync_to_async`. In Flask, do NOT expect concurrency from `async def` views under WSGI — they run inside a sync wrapper and provide no parallelism. In FastAPI, all I/O on the request path must be `await`ed; never call sync `requests.get()` or sync ORM inside `async def`.
rule.rationale: Calling sync ORM inside a Django async view blocks the event loop thread. Flask's `async def` support exists for ergonomics but provides no real concurrency under WSGI. FastAPI's perf advantage evaporates the moment a sync call lands inside an `async def` handler.

rule.statement: Record the framework choice in `constitution.md` (or equivalent ADR) as a single line of the form `framework = django|fastapi|flask` plus a paragraph naming the trigger rule (r1, r2, or r3). Downstream agents MUST refuse to switch frameworks mid-feature; switching requires a new ADR superseding the previous.
rule.rationale: Mixing framework idioms (`@app.route` in FastAPI, `User.objects` outside a Django process) is a top-3 LLM failure mode. A pinned ADR plus a constitution lock makes the failure detectable in code review.
rule.reference: Interactive picker that produces the ADR line.
